{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />

Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== <references group=note/>
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:Kibitsu-Zukuri}}

revision.text:
{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== <references group=note/>
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:Kibitsu-Zukuri}}

The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== <references group=note/>

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== <references group=note/>

The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Kibitsu-zukuri]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kibitsu-zukuri]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Kibitsu-zukuri]] [[Category:New]]

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== <references group=note/>

The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]

Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />

{{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== <references group=note/>

Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. {{short description|Style of Shinto architecture}} {{italic title}} [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 10 cropped.jpg|275 px|thumb|Kibitsu Shrine's ''honden''-''haiden'' complex. The main entrance (hidden) is on the right]] {{nihongo|'''''Kibitsu-zukuri'''''|吉備津造}}, {{nihongo|''kibi-zukuri''|吉備造}} or {{nihongo|''hiyoku irimoya-zukuri''||入母屋造|paired wing hip-and-gable roof style}} is a traditional Japanese [[Shinto architecture|Shinto architectural]] style characterized by four [[dormer]] [[gable]]s, two per lateral side, on the roof of a very large ''[[honden]]'' (sanctuary).<ref name="jaanus"/> The gables are set at a right angle to the main roof ridge, and the ''honden'' is part of a single complex also including a ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' (worship hall). [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]] in [[Okayama, Okayama|Okayama]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], [[Japan]] is the sole example of the style, although the Soshi-[[Dō (architecture)|dō]] of [[Hokekyō-ji (Ichikawa)|Hokekyō-ji]] in [[Chiba prefecture]] is believed to have been modeled on it.<ref name=jaanus>{{Jaanus2|k/kibitsuzukuri|Kibitsu-zukuri|August 19, 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |title=Architectural Preservation in Japan |author=Larsen, Knut Einar |publisher=[[ICOMOS]] International Wood Committee |year=1994 |pages=121–5 |ISBN=82-519-1432-9}}</ref>
==Structure== [[File:Kibitsu Jinja honden haiden floorplan.svg|thumb|left|The shrine's floorplan]] The T-shaped shrine is composed of two buildings: the ''haiden'' or prayer hall, in the front, and the ''[[honden]]'' or sanctuary, in the back, both under the same roof and joined by a short stairway (see floorplan). Both buildings show the clear influence of [[Japanese Buddhist architecture|Buddhist architecture]], as they include features of all major styles, that is [[Daibutsuyō]], [[Zenshūyō]] and [[Wayō]].<ref name=jaanus/>
===''Honden''=== The ''honden'', which shows strong ''[[daibutsuyō]]'' influences, is extremely large, measuring 14.64 x 17.99 m, or 5 (front) x 8 (depth) x 7 (rear) [[Ken (architecture)|bays]], with bays of a different length according to their position.<ref name=jaanus/>
The ''honden's'' interior has a complex structure, being divided in six separate sections joined by six different stairways (see flooplan). At the very center of the ''honden'' are two sanctuaries, the {{nihongo|''nai-naijin''|内内陣|inner inner shrine}} which measures 3 x 2 bays, and the {{nihongo|''naijin''|内陣|inner shrine}}, which measures 3 x 1 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> The two sanctuaries are surrounded on all sides by two corridors called the {{nihongo|''chūjin''|中陣|intermediate shrine}} and the {{nihongo|''gejin''|外陣|external shrine}}. Between the ''chūjin'' and the ''gejin'' lies a 5 x 1 bay space called {{nihongo|''kōhai-no-ma''|向拝間|lit. toward prayer space}},<ref group=note>A ''kōhai'' is a pent roof above the stairs of a shrine or temple</ref> also called {{nihongo|''ake-no-dan''| 朱壇|crimson platform}}. The closer one gets to the center, the higher the floor and the ceiling.<ref name=kj>{{cite web|title=Honden |url=http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |work=Kibitsu Jinja's Official Site |publisher=Kibitsu Jinja |access-date=25 August 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110823153128/http://kibitujinja.com/honden.html |archive-date=23 August 2011 }}</ref> The ceiling's structure itself changes, as most of the ''chūjin'' and the entire ''gejin'' have no ceiling, and the roof is therefore exposed, whereas other sections have ceilings of different types. The ''nainaijin'' for example lies below the gables.<ref name="kj"/> The whole area is decorated with vermillion and black [[lacquer]].<ref name=jaanus/> [[File:Kibitsu Jinja 04.JPG|thumb|upright|The ''haiden''. The main entrance is on the left.]]
===''Haiden''=== The ''honden'' is connected in the front to the ''haiden'' by a 1 x 1 bay passage and a short stairway. The ''haiden's'' [[Moya (architecture)|core]] is just 3 x 1 bays, but it is surrounded on three sides by a 1-bay wide ''[[mokoshi]]'' (pent roof), bringing the building's external dimensions to 4 x 4 bays.<ref name=jaanus/> Both entrances to the ''haiden'' are on the gabled side (''[[tsumairi]]'' style).
===Roof=== Together with the outsize ''honden'', the most visible feature of the shrine are the twin gables on both sides of the roof. This style of roof, called ''hiyoku [[irimoya-zukuri]]'', or "paired wing, hip-and-gable roof style", consists of two ridges at a right angle to the main roof which end in two dormer gables.<ref name=jaanus />
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]]
==Notes== <references group=note/>
==References== {{Reflist}}
